play.description: Victor Wembanyama's third-year transformation reached new heights in his first taste of NBA postseason action. Making his playoff debut as the San Antonio Spurs opened their first-round series against Portland, the towering center dominated from the opening tip with a masterful two-way performance. Here he goes coast-to-coast, behind-the-back, rises above the Trail Blazers defense and throws down an emphatic slam, punctuating what would become a historic 35-point showcase on ridiculous 61.9% shooting and 5-of-6 from three-point range in the 111-98 victory.

Victor Wembanyama's shot-blocking ability transforms entire possessions, and Zion Williamson learned that firsthand. Early in the first quarter at Smoothie King Center, the Pelicans star drove hard to the basket attempting his signature reverse layup. The 7-foot-4 phenom read the play perfectly, sliding over to meet Williamson at the rim and sending the shot away with authority. Wembanyama nearly recorded a triple-double with 29 points, 11 rebounds and 9 blocks in San Antonio's 120-116 overtime victory on October 24, 2025.

Victor Wembanyama and Maxime Raynaud grew up in France playing the same sport, dreaming of the same league, and on February 21, 2026, they shared the Moody Center floor in Austin together. Fox drives the paint and kicks to Barnes, Barnes one-touches it to Wembanyama at the three-point line, and the 7-foot-4 phenom gives Raynaud a head fake left before going right and rising for a windmill slam. The Spurs won their eighth straight, 139-122, with Wembanyama finishing with 28 points, 15 rebounds, six assists and four blocks.

Victor Wembanyama's combination of length and athleticism creates highlight plays few others can replicate. Early in the third quarter of a tight November 14, 2025 contest, the Spurs center caught the ball near the rim, tosses it off the backoard to himself and hammered home a dunk that gave San Antonio a 52-51 lead. The Frost Bank Center erupted as Wembanyama imposed his will on both ends throughout the night. The French phenom tallied 26 points, 12 rebounds, 4 assists and 3 blocks across 38 minutes, converting 3-of-8 from deep in the narrow 109-108 defeat.

Victor Wembanyama has already established himself as one of the league's most versatile big men, but clutch moments like this define franchise cornerstones. With the Spurs trailing 100-101 and just 1.1 seconds remaining, the third-year center pulls up from 17 feet and buries a pullup jumper over Phoenix defenders to give San Antonio the lead. This shot didn't just win the game, it clinched the Spurs' first playoff berth since 2019, ending a seven-year drought and sending the Frost Bank Center into a frenzy.

Victor Wembanyama combines unprecedented length with explosive athleticism that defies physics. The Spurs' generational talent rose up for a thunderous rim-rattling slam on Christmas Day, showcasing the unique skill set that has the basketball world captivated. Wemby attacked the rim with authority and flushed it home with both hands, energizing his squad en route to 19 points and 11 rebounds. The 7-foot-4 phenom shot 6-for-12 from the field in San Antonio's 117-102 road victory on December 25, 2025.

Victor Wembanyama's combination of size and athleticism makes him a nightmare matchup, especially when he's soaring above the rim. In a matchup of #1 picks, Wemby lets Cooper Flagg know it's his house with a huge skying rejection, but he wasn't done there. Moments later, Devin Vassell spotted Wemby cutting to the basket and lofted a perfect alley-oop pass. The 7-foot-4 phenom caught it in stride and hammered home a jam that brought the home crowd to its feet. Wembanyama posted a double-double with 16 points and 11 rebounds in San Antonio's dominant 138-125 victory over Dallas on February 7, 2026.

With the Spurs building momentum at home on January 15, 2026, the 7'4" center sets off an elite two-way sequence. After denying Ryan Rollins at the rim, Wemby gets the ball to his sophomore gaurd to push the pace and then quickly finds himself back with the ball from 30 feet out. Wembanyama immediately rises up with the ball tracing a perfect arc through the net for his fifth three-pointer, part of a 22-point, 10-rebound double-double performance that powered San Antonio to a 119-101 victory.

Victor Wembanyama once again showed why he’s redefining the limits of a 7-foot-4 center. With one minute left and San Antonio trailing by three at the United Center, Stephon Castle pushed the break and found Wemby on the right wing. On the move, Wembanyama calmly buried a 27-footer to tie the game—but that shot was only the beginning.

On the ensuing possession, he erased Chicago at the rim with a clutch block, then sealed the comeback on the other end by isolating, crossing his defender, and drilling a step-back three to put the game away. Wembanyama finished with 38 points, 12 rebounds, 5 blocks, and a blistering 6-for-9 from deep, powering the Spurs to a 121–117 comeback win on November 10, 2025.

With an offensive repertoire built to score in any scenario, Victor Wembanyama has shown his opponents everything but mercy. Calmly navigating through a sea of red on a pick-and-roll, the San Antonio Spurs big man finds a seam through the middle of the floor before taking flight for a violent, one-handed contact flush at the rim against the Houston Rockets. Adding yet another clip to his rapidly-growing rookie highlight reel, Wembanyama was all-encompassing in the loss on December 11, 2023, with 15 points, 18 rebounds, three assists, and five blocks.

With a single stretch of his arm, Victor Wembanyama helped ensure both a short-term win for the San Antonio Spurs, and a long-term win for his Rookie of the Year case. Just a few sequences removed from a string of back-to-back 3-pointers to catapult his team into a nine-point edge, the dazzling first-year star turns his attention to the more thankless side of the floor, mirroring a similarly-talented Oklahoma City Thunder big on his drive before blocking his shot and recovering the attempt in one motion. Rising to the occasion in one of the 2023-24 NBA season’s most anticipated duels, Wembanyama embedded his name into league history in recording 28 points, 13 rebounds, seven assists, two steals, five blocks, and five 3-pointers, the latter helping it become the first statline of its kind. As the cherry on top, the Spurs came out victorious against one of the Association’s elites on February 29, 2024.

In between giving his teammates plenty of reasons for celebratory, end-of-game high-fives on Halloween night, Victor Wembanyama’s box score confirmed that he also had a different type of five-by-five in mind. Seemingly dedicating Thursday’s battle to accomplishing tasks that 7-foot-4 players aren’t supposed to be able to, Wembanyama gifted Salt Lake City with sweet on-court treats throughout October 31, 2024’s win. Excellence arrived almost immediately, as Wembanyama helped the San Antonio Spurs shake free of the metaphorically-fitting “cobwebs,” erasing a slow start through a rebound, followed by an audacious coast-to-coast pull-up triple. Far from finished, the superstar in silver-and-black offered his trademark assortment of abnormally-normal acts. Defending both the ballhandler and screener in the pick-and-roll? Using his all-reaching eight-foot wingspan to both pickpocket point guards and deliver behind-the-back feeds to teammates in transition? Joining “The Point God” for spiritual experiences through above-the-rim alley-oop connections? The second-year big had it all, and did it all against the Utah Jazz, becoming just league history’s third-ever player to record multiple five-by-five displays. Ironically costumed as “Slenderman” upon arrival, Wembanyama’s anything-but-slim statline finished as follows: 25 points (on 9-of-20 shooting), nine rebounds, seven assists, five steals, five blocks, four trifectas, and a +43 plus-minus.

Twenty-five points before the first quarter horn sounds. That was the opening statement Victor Wembanyama delivered at Crypto.com Arena on February 10, 2026, a franchise record for a Spurs player in a single quarter and the third-highest scoring frame in team history behind only George Gervin and David Robinson. He set the tone early in the half court: Fox finds him on the left wing, and Wembanyama uses a crafty spin move to blow past Jake LaRavia and throw it down, then Castle hits him on the right wing for a catch-and-shoot triple that a 7-foot-4 player should have no business draining so clean, then Castle feeds him in the post for a turnaround jumper over Maxi Kleber. By halftime, his 37 points were the most by a Spur in the 21st century. He checked out midway through the third having played just 26 minutes, the fifth time in shot clock era history a player reached 40 in that few. His sixth career 40-point game moved him one past Tim Duncan in franchise history. He finished with 40 points, 12 rebounds, two steals and one block in the 136-108 victory.

Just when you think you’ve seen everything there is to see during Victor Wembanyama’s otherworldly rookie season, the San Antonio Spurs rookie merely uses his 7-foot-4 frame to raise the bar even higher. In a perfect encapsulation of almost everything Year One has offered, Wembanyama introduced the Los Angeles Lakers to everything but the kitchen sink. Vulture-like chasedown blocks that looked eerily similar to those of his Hall of Fame opponents? Check. Off-the-dribble drives and stepback 3-point bombs? You got it. All-encompassing court vision and improved playmaking? Of course. Constructing a box score befitting of both the size of his frame and his potential, the 2023 No. 1 pick poured in 27 points, 10 rebounds, eight assists, five steals, five blocks, and three 3-pointers in the loss on February 23, 2024. A walking record-breaker waiting to happen, Wembanyama became just NBA history’s second rookie to achieve a 5x5 game, and No. 22 all-time.

To become NBA history’s No. 1 producer in a specific stat after just 111 career games is almost unheard of, but during January 31, 2025’s head-to-head against a fellow global icon, Victor Wembanyama again discovered that, with 96-inch wingspans, very little can be considered completely “out of reach.” Tested immediately against the always-physical Giannis Antetokounmpo — collector of a league-high 19.5 points per game in the paint alone — Wembanyama rose to the occasion quite literally, rejecting the Milwaukee Bucks’ megastar during a two-swats-in-14-seconds sequence. On pace for a shot-blocking sensation before the game’s literal first commercial break, Wembanyama, appropriately, kept his antennas raised, patrolling the paint through from-behind denials, near top-of-the-backboard send aways, and equal offensive brilliance. Admittedly bothered by allergies since returning from the NBA’s Paris Games, the recently-named All-Star sprung his way into a 30-point gem (on 9-of-20 shooting), joined by 14 rebounds, six blocks, five 3-pointers, and a game-high 13 shot contests. Alongside a victory, the breathtaking box score allowed the San Antonio Spurs’ second-year talent to record league history’s most games with at least five 3-pointers and five blocks, with five such instances.

With every flip of the calendar, there stands the realistic chance of Victor Wembanyama —  sometimes through on-court excellence, and at other times on the box score — offering something the NBA world has seldom seen before. On November 13, 2024, in the highest-scoring game of his career to date, the San Antonio Spurs’ French phenom found the necessary avenues to accomplish both at once. Slightly delayed in his arrival as the final player downfloor, yet promptly on time for the celebration of a new career-high, Wembanyama lets fly an audacious 31-footer, marking both his eighth trifecta, and a 18-point lead for San Antonio. Helped somewhat by the Washington Wizards’ never-say-die comeback attempt, Wembanyama parlayed additional opportunities into a single-game scoring total for the record books. In just 32 minutes time, the 7-foot-4 star’s lighting of the scoreboards confirmed a 50-point masterpiece, coronating him as NBA history’s fourth-youngest to achieve such a feat, as well as its tallest. Ever the stat sheet stuffer, Wembanyama’s 50-piece was accompanied by eyebrow-raising efficiency (18-of-29), alongside six rebounds, two assists, one steal, three blocks, and six 3-pointers.

Having already exceeded any expectation lofty enough to rival his 7-foot-3 frame on the NBA’s landscape, it likely came as no surprise that Victor Wembanyama rose to the pressures of his France homecoming with relative ease. Supercharged by the immediate, well-deserved standing ovation from the 15,935 in attendance at Accor Arena, Wembanyama’s on-court “bonjour” of sorts didn’t take long to arrive. Over a 32-minute showing, those viewing were introduced to the full “Victor Wembanyama Experience.” From midcourt logo 3-pointers to emphatic swats, to even alley-oops — including one to himself — the home game of a different type reaffirmed what was already known: the San Antonio Spurs’ second-year big remains, fittingly, on the road to superstardom. Requiring a moment or two to get fully calibrated, the Spurs rallied behind No. 1, turning his energy into a second-half runaway, and subsequently, a 30-point win over the Indiana Pacers on January 23, 2025. Packing versatility into his suitcase, Wembanyama’s performance was, in a word, “grande,” as he accumulated 30 points (on 13-of-21 shooting), 11 rebounds, six assists, one steal, five blocks, and four 3-pointers.

Amidst all of the excitement surrounding perhaps the most highly-anticipated NBA rookie season post-2003, Victor Wembanyama, in short time, has already become a man of many nicknames, adjectives, and superlatives. For some, “Wemby” or “The Extraterrestrial” do the trick; for others, perhaps “the future of the NBA” fits the bill more demonstratively. Yet, given the many acts that defined Wembanyama’s 2023-24, perhaps there’s room for one more: “the first player to ever …” Perfectly befitting of a player with a 7-foot-4 frame, an 8-foot wingspan, and a skillset built for every need from one-through-five, Wembanyama’s impact knew no limits in Year One. The immediate accolades included becoming the sixth-ever unanimous Rookie of the Year, the Defensive Player of the Year runner-up, and First Team All-Defense selection. Though, across a 71-game showcase unlike anything ever seen, Wembanyama’s reach also allowed for 10-block triple-doubles, rare five-by-five games, and just the league’s second instance of 200 blocks and 100 3-pointers. Evoking comparisons to franchise legends of the past, the San Antonio Spurs’ star unicorn precursored the ensuing decade through 21.4 points, 10.6 rebounds, 3.9 assists, 1.2 steals, 3.6 blocks — including an NBA-high 342 combined “stocks” —  and 47-33-80 percentages.

On May 16, 2023, five long months before Victor Wembanyama’s now well-known name would become a commonality over the San Antonio Spurs’ loudspeakers, there remained a sense that the 2023 NBA Draft Lottery would hold a special significance with the “most hyped prospect in two decades” being up for grabs. With little doubt over who would be selected No. 1, the 7-foot-4, do-it-all marvel has fittingly rewarded the Alamo City’s faith by giving them little to doubt about his potential over the coming months. Putting his Defensive Player of the Year-caliber traits and All-Star potential on display from Day One, Wembanyama introduced himself to both the Dallas Mavericks and the NBA’s history books with face-up jumpers, 3-point shooting of every variation, polite pleasantries around the rim, and defensive statements, commanding both the attention and awe of his new fanbase. Matching the lofty expectations with a Texas-sized impact, the rookie star recorded 15 points, five rebounds, two assists, two steals, and one block in just 23 minutes of action in his debut on October 25, 2023.
